chapters.title: What is DINOv3?
chapters.narrative:
DINOv3 is a self-supervised vision model from Meta AI that builds on the DINO/DINOv2 family. It trains a Vision Transformer (ViT) to produce universal visual features without labels, using self-distillation between a student and teacher network.

Key differences from DINOv2:
• Rotary Position Embedding (RoPE) replaces learned positional embeddings, enabling flexible resolution handling
• A new Gram Loss regularizes patch-level features to prevent representation collapse
• Sinkhorn-Knopp normalization replaces simple centering for the teacher
• Storage tokens (evolved from DINOv2's register tokens) provide global memory slots
• SwiGLU feed-forward networks and RMSNorm as optional architecture upgrades

The codebase lives in `dinov3/` with models, layers, loss functions, and a training meta-architecture (`SSLMetaArch`) that orchestrates the full student-teacher pipeline.

chapters.title: Step 1: Image → Patch Tokens
chapters.narrative:
The first stage converts a raw image into a sequence of patch tokens. A Conv2d with kernel_size = patch_size acts as a learned linear projection: it slides over the image in non-overlapping windows, producing one embedding vector per patch.

For a 224×224 image with patch_size=16, this yields a 14×14 grid = 196 patch tokens, each of dimension embed_dim (e.g., 768 for ViT-Base).

code_blocks.label: PatchEmbed — Conv2d as a learned projection
code_blocks.source:
class PatchEmbed(nn.Module):
    """
    2D image to patch embedding: (B,C,H,W) -> (B,N,D)
    """

    def __init__(
        self,
        img_size: Union[int, Tuple[int, int]] = 224,
        patch_size: Union[int, Tuple[int, int]] = 16,
        in_chans: int = 3,
        embed_dim: int = 768,
        norm_layer: Callable | None = None,
        flatten_embedding: bool = True,
    ) -> None:
        super().__init__()

        image_HW = make_2tuple(img_size)
        patch_HW = make_2tuple(patch_size)
        patch_grid_size = (
            image_HW[0] // patch_HW[0],
            image_HW[1] // patch_HW[1],
        )

        self.img_size = image_HW
        self.patch_size = patch_HW
        self.patches_resolution = patch_grid_size
        self.num_patches = patch_grid_size[0] * patch_grid_size[1]

        self.in_chans = in_chans
        self.embed_dim = embed_dim

        self.flatten_embedding = flatten_embedding

        self.proj = nn.Conv2d(in_chans, embed_dim, kernel_size=patch_HW, stride=patch_HW)
        self.norm = norm_layer(embed_dim) if norm_layer else nn.Identity()

annotations.explanation: Computes the grid size: for 224px image with 16px patches → (14, 14) = 196 total patches. This grid shape is used later for positional embedding.

The core projection: a Conv2d with kernel=patch_size and stride=patch_size. This is mathematically equivalent to splitting the image into non-overlapping patches and applying a linear projection to each — but implemented as a single efficient convolution. Optional normalization follows.

code_blocks.label: PatchEmbed.forward() — the actual conversion
code_blocks.source:
def forward(self, x: Tensor) -> Tensor:
        _, _, H, W = x.shape
        x = self.proj(x)  # B C H W
        H, W = x.size(2), x.size(3)
        x = x.flatten(2).transpose(1, 2)  # B HW C
        x = self.norm(x)
        if not self.flatten_embedding:
            x = x.reshape(-1, H, W, self.embed_dim)  # B H W C
        return x

annotations.explanation: Conv2d produces (B, embed_dim, H/patch, W/patch). Then flatten(2) merges the spatial dims into a sequence, and transpose gives (B, num_patches, embed_dim) — the standard transformer input format.

chapters.title: Step 2: The Vision Transformer Backbone
chapters.narrative:
The DinoVisionTransformer is the backbone of DINOv3. Compared to a vanilla ViT, it has several key upgrades:

• RoPE (Rotary Position Embedding) instead of learned absolute positional embeddings — this allows the model to generalize to different resolutions at inference time
• Storage tokens — learnable tokens prepended to the sequence that act as global memory (evolved from DINOv2's register tokens)
• A mask token for iBOT-style masked image modeling
• Support for untied normalization between CLS and patch tokens

code_blocks.label: DinoVisionTransformer.__init__ — key components
code_blocks.source:
self.patch_embed = PatchEmbed(
            img_size=img_size,
            patch_size=patch_size,
            in_chans=in_chans,
            embed_dim=embed_dim,
            flatten_embedding=False,
        )

        self.cls_token = nn.Parameter(torch.empty(1, 1, embed_dim, device=device))
        self.n_storage_tokens = n_storage_tokens
        if self.n_storage_tokens > 0:
            self.storage_tokens = nn.Parameter(torch.empty(1, n_storage_tokens, embed_dim, device=device))

annotations.explanation: PatchEmbed with flatten_embedding=False returns (B, H, W, D) instead of (B, N, D). The spatial dimensions are preserved for RoPE, which needs to know the 2D grid structure.

CLS token is a single learnable vector prepended to the sequence — it aggregates global information via attention. Storage tokens (when n_storage_tokens > 0) are additional learnable tokens that serve as global 'scratch pads' the model can read/write to.

code_blocks.label: RoPE instead of learned positional embeddings
code_blocks.source:
self.rope_embed = RopePositionEmbedding(
            embed_dim=embed_dim,
            num_heads=num_heads,
            base=pos_embed_rope_base,
            min_period=pos_embed_rope_min_period,
            max_period=pos_embed_rope_max_period,
            normalize_coords=pos_embed_rope_normalize_coords,
            shift_coords=pos_embed_rope_shift_coords,
            jitter_coords=pos_embed_rope_jitter_coords,
            rescale_coords=pos_embed_rope_rescale_coords,
            dtype=dtype_dict[pos_embed_rope_dtype],
            device=device,
        )

annotations.explanation: RoPE (Rotary Position Embedding) encodes position by rotating Q and K vectors in attention. Unlike learned embeddings, RoPE generalizes to unseen resolutions — the model can be trained at 224px and applied to 512px. It also supports coordinate jittering and rescaling as data augmentation during training.

code_blocks.label: Building the transformer blocks
code_blocks.source:
blocks_list = [
            SelfAttentionBlock(
                dim=embed_dim,
                num_heads=num_heads,
                ffn_ratio=ffn_ratio_sequence[i],
                qkv_bias=qkv_bias,
                proj_bias=proj_bias,
                ffn_bias=ffn_bias,
                drop_path=drop_path_rate,
                norm_layer=norm_layer_cls,
                act_layer=nn.GELU,
                ffn_layer=ffn_layer_cls,
                init_values=layerscale_init,
                mask_k_bias=mask_k_bias,
                device=device,
            )
            for i in range(depth)
        ]

        self.chunked_blocks = False
        self.blocks = nn.ModuleList(blocks_list)

annotations.explanation: Creates `depth` identical transformer blocks (e.g., 12 for ViT-Base, 40 for ViT-Giant). Each block contains self-attention + FFN with configurable FFN type (standard MLP or SwiGLU), normalization (LayerNorm or RMSNorm), and optional LayerScale. Note: all blocks share the same drop_path rate — the sample-based DropPath inside the block handles stochastic depth differently from standard DropPath.

chapters.title: Step 3: Self-Attention with RoPE
chapters.narrative:
The attention mechanism in DINOv3 is standard multi-head self-attention, but with one key difference: position information is injected via RoPE rather than added to the input embeddings.

RoPE works by rotating the query and key vectors based on their position before computing the dot product. The mathematical effect is that the attention score between two tokens depends on their relative position, not absolute position. This makes the model resolution-agnostic.

The actual attention computation uses PyTorch's fused scaled_dot_product_attention (Flash Attention when available) for efficiency.

code_blocks.label: SelfAttention — QKV projection + RoPE + Flash Attention
code_blocks.source:
class SelfAttention(nn.Module):
    def __init__(
        self,
        dim: int,
        num_heads: int = 8,
        qkv_bias: bool = False,
        proj_bias: bool = True,
        attn_drop: float = 0.0,
        proj_drop: float = 0.0,
        mask_k_bias: bool = False,
        device=None,
    ) -> None:
        super().__init__()
        self.num_heads = num_heads
        head_dim = dim // num_heads
        self.scale = head_dim**-0.5

        linear_class = LinearKMaskedBias if mask_k_bias else nn.Linear
        self.qkv = linear_class(dim, dim * 3, bias=qkv_bias, device=device)
        self.attn_drop = nn.Dropout(attn_drop)
        self.proj = nn.Linear(dim, dim, bias=proj_bias, device=device)
        self.proj_drop = nn.Dropout(proj_drop)

annotations.explanation: Standard attention scaling factor 1/√(d_head). head_dim = dim // num_heads, e.g., 768/12 = 64 for ViT-Base.

Fused QKV projection — a single Linear layer outputs 3× the dimension, which is then split into Q, K, V. This is more efficient than three separate projections. mask_k_bias is a DINOv3-specific option that zeros out the bias for the K portion of QKV.

code_blocks.label: compute_attention — where RoPE is applied
code_blocks.source:
def compute_attention(self, qkv: Tensor, attn_bias=None, rope=None) -> Tensor:
        assert attn_bias is None
        B, N, _ = qkv.shape
        C = self.qkv.in_features

        qkv = qkv.reshape(B, N, 3, self.num_heads, C // self.num_heads)
        q, k, v = torch.unbind(qkv, 2)
        q, k, v = [t.transpose(1, 2) for t in [q, k, v]]
        if rope is not None:
            q, k = self.apply_rope(q, k, rope)
        x = torch.nn.functional.scaled_dot_product_attention(q, k, v)
        x = x.transpose(1, 2)
        return x.reshape([B, N, C])

annotations.explanation: Reshape fused QKV into separate Q, K, V tensors. The reshape creates (B, N, 3, heads, head_dim), then unbind splits along dim=2. transpose(1,2) gives (B, heads, N, head_dim) for batched attention.

RoPE is applied ONLY to Q and K (not V). It rotates the vectors based on their 2D spatial position. CLS and storage tokens (which have no spatial position) are left unrotated — they're in a 'prefix' that RoPE skips.
PyTorch's fused attention kernel — dispatches to Flash Attention v2, Memory-Efficient Attention, or standard attention depending on hardware. Flash Attention is O(N) memory instead of O(N²).

chapters.title: Step 4: The Transformer Block
chapters.narrative:
Each transformer block follows the pre-norm pattern: LayerNorm → Attention → Residual → LayerNorm → FFN → Residual. DINOv3 adds two features:

• LayerScale — learnable per-channel scaling applied to residuals, initialized to a small value. This stabilizes training of deep transformers.
• Sample-based DropPath — instead of dropping individual tokens, a random subset of batch samples skip the residual branch entirely. This is a unique form of stochastic depth.

During inference (sample_drop_ratio = 0), the block simplifies to the standard pre-norm transformer.

code_blocks.label: SelfAttentionBlock — the inference path (clean version)
code_blocks.source:
else:
            x_attn = x + self.ls1(self.attn(self.norm1(x), rope=rope))
            x_ffn = x_attn + self.ls2(self.mlp(self.norm2(x_attn)))

        return x_ffn

annotations.explanation: The clean inference path: (1) norm1 → attention → LayerScale → residual, then (2) norm2 → MLP → LayerScale → residual. ls1/ls2 are LayerScale modules (or Identity if init_values=None). This is a standard pre-norm transformer block with optional LayerScale.

code_blocks.label: Sample-based DropPath — DINOv3's unique stochastic depth
code_blocks.source:
if self.training and self.sample_drop_ratio > 0.0:
            indices_1 = (torch.randperm(b, device=x.device))[:sample_subset_size]

            x_subset_1 = x[indices_1]
            rope_subset = self._maybe_index_rope(rope, indices_1)
            residual_1 = self.attn(self.norm1(x_subset_1), rope=rope_subset)

            x_attn = torch.index_add(
                x,
                dim=0,
                source=self.ls1(residual_1),
                index=indices_1,
                alpha=residual_scale_factor,
            )

annotations.explanation: Instead of dropping paths per-token, DINOv3 randomly selects a SUBSET of batch samples to process. Only these samples go through attention; the rest keep their input unchanged. This saves FLOPs during training.

torch.index_add is the key trick: it adds the attention output back to the FULL batch tensor at the selected indices, scaled by b/subset_size to compensate for the subsampling. Samples not in the subset keep their original values — their residual connection is effectively the identity.

chapters.title: Step 5: The Full Forward Pass
chapters.narrative:
The forward pass processes multiple crops (global + local) jointly. Each crop goes through patch embedding, gets CLS and storage tokens prepended, then runs through all transformer blocks with RoPE. The output separates CLS tokens (global features) from patch tokens (spatial features).

A key detail: the model supports processing a LIST of inputs with different resolutions in one pass — this is essential for DINO's multi-crop training strategy where global crops (224px) and local crops (96px) are processed together.

code_blocks.label: prepare_tokens_with_masks — assembling the input sequence

annotations.explanation: Patch embed returns (B, H, W, D) since flatten_embedding=False. Flatten(1,2) merges spatial dims into sequence: (B, H*W, D). The (H, W) tuple is saved for RoPE, which needs the 2D grid shape.

For iBOT masked image modeling: if masks are provided, masked patches are REPLACED with the learned mask_token. The trick '0 * self.mask_token' in the else branch ensures mask_token gets gradients even when no mask is used (otherwise it would never be updated).

Assemble the full sequence: [CLS, storage_tokens, patch_tokens]. The order matters — CLS is at position 0, storage tokens follow, then patch tokens. RoPE is only applied to patch tokens (it skips the prefix of CLS + storage tokens).

code_blocks.label: forward_features_list — processing multi-crop inputs

annotations.explanation: Each crop (global 224px and local 96px) is prepared independently — different crops have different numbers of patches and different (H,W) grid shapes. This list of tensors is the input to the transformer.

The core loop: for each block, compute RoPE sin/cos for each crop's grid size, then pass ALL crops through the block together. The block's forward_list method concatenates the crops for efficient batched computation, then unconcatenates the results.

chapters.title: Step 6: DINO Loss — Self-Distillation
chapters.narrative:
DINOv3 trains without labels using self-distillation: a student network sees masked/augmented views while a teacher network (EMA of student) sees clean views. The student learns to match the teacher's output distribution.

The critical difference from DINOv2's loss is the use of Sinkhorn-Knopp normalization instead of simple centering. Sinkhorn-Knopp iteratively normalizes the teacher's output matrix to be doubly-stochastic (rows and columns both sum to uniform), providing a stronger anti-collapse mechanism.

The loss itself is cross-entropy between the teacher's sharpened distribution and the student's softmax logits, computed across all student-teacher crop pairs.

code_blocks.label: Sinkhorn-Knopp teacher centering — replacing simple EMA centering
code_blocks.source:
@torch.no_grad()
    def sinkhorn_knopp_teacher(self, teacher_output, teacher_temp, n_iterations=3):
        teacher_output = teacher_output.float()
        world_size = get_subgroup_size() if dist.is_initialized() else 1
        Q = torch.exp(teacher_output / teacher_temp).t()  # K-by-B
        B = Q.shape[1] * world_size
        K = Q.shape[0]  # how many prototypes

        # make the matrix sums to 1
        sum_Q = torch.sum(Q)
        if dist.is_initialized():
            dist.all_reduce(sum_Q, group=get_process_subgroup())
        Q /= sum_Q

        for _ in range(n_iterations):
            # normalize each row: total weight per prototype must be 1/K
            sum_of_rows = torch.sum(Q, dim=1, keepdim=True)
            if dist.is_initialized():
                dist.all_reduce(sum_of_rows, group=get_process_subgroup())
            Q /= sum_of_rows
            Q /= K

            # normalize each column: total weight per sample must be 1/B
            Q /= torch.sum(Q, dim=0, keepdim=True)
            Q /= B

        Q *= B  # the columns must sum to 1 so that Q is an assignment
        return Q.t()

annotations.explanation: Exponentiate teacher logits divided by temperature, then transpose to K×B (prototypes × batch). This converts logits into a soft assignment matrix where each entry represents how strongly sample b is assigned to prototype k.

The Sinkhorn-Knopp algorithm: 3 iterations of alternating row/column normalization. Row normalization ensures each prototype gets equal total assignment (prevents prototype collapse). Column normalization ensures each sample gets equal total probability (prevents sample collapse). The all_reduce calls handle distributed training across GPUs.
Scale columns to sum to 1, making Q a proper probability distribution over prototypes for each sample. The transpose gives B×K — one probability vector per sample in the batch.

code_blocks.label: DINOLoss.forward — cross-entropy across all crop pairs

annotations.explanation: Student logits are divided by student_temp (0.1) and passed through log_softmax. Higher temperature → softer distribution. Using log_softmax (not softmax) because cross-entropy needs log probabilities.

Elegant einsum: 's b k, t b k -> ' computes cross-entropy between EVERY student crop and EVERY teacher crop, summed over all. This is the DINO multi-crop loss — student local crops learn from teacher global crops and vice versa.

When ignore_diagonal=True: compute per-crop-pair losses (s t matrix), then zero out the diagonal. This prevents a crop from learning from its own augmented view — forcing the model to learn cross-view invariance.

chapters.title: Step 7: The DINO Projection Head
chapters.narrative:
Between the backbone and the loss, there's a projection head that maps the backbone's output features to a space where the DINO loss operates. This is a multi-layer MLP with a bottleneck, followed by L2 normalization and a final linear layer (no bias) that projects to the 'prototype' dimension.

The L2 normalization before the last layer is critical — it projects features onto a unit hypersphere, which interacts well with the Sinkhorn-Knopp normalization in the loss.

code_blocks.label: DINOHead — MLP + normalize + prototype projection
code_blocks.source:
class DINOHead(nn.Module):
    def __init__(
        self,
        in_dim,
        out_dim,
        use_bn=False,
        nlayers=3,
        hidden_dim=2048,
        bottleneck_dim=256,
        mlp_bias=True,
    ):
        super().__init__()
        nlayers = max(nlayers, 1)
        self.mlp = _build_mlp(
            nlayers,
            in_dim,
            bottleneck_dim,
            hidden_dim=hidden_dim,
            use_bn=use_bn,
            bias=mlp_bias,
        )
        self.last_layer = nn.Linear(bottleneck_dim, out_dim, bias=False)

    def forward(self, x, no_last_layer=False, only_last_layer=False):
        if not only_last_layer:
            x = self.mlp(x)
            eps = 1e-6 if x.dtype == torch.float16 else 1e-12
            x = nn.functional.normalize(x, dim=-1, p=2, eps=eps)
        if not no_last_layer:
            x = self.last_layer(x)
        return x

annotations.explanation: The MLP is a 3-layer network: in_dim → hidden_dim(2048) → hidden_dim → bottleneck_dim(256), with GELU activations. This progressively compresses the representation.
The final projection has NO bias — this is important because it maps from the L2-normalized bottleneck to the prototype space. The columns of this weight matrix act as prototype vectors.

L2 normalization projects the bottleneck features onto a unit hypersphere. The eps value adapts to dtype (1e-6 for fp16, 1e-12 for fp32) to avoid division by zero. This normalization is what makes the subsequent dot-product attention in the loss work as cosine similarity.

summary: DINOv3 is a self-supervised Vision Transformer from Meta AI. It uses RoPE positional embeddings for resolution-flexible attention, Sinkhorn-Knopp normalization for anti-collapse teacher centering, storage tokens as global memory, and a multi-crop self-distillation objective. The student learns to match the teacher's sharpened output distribution across different image crops. Key architectural choices include sample-based DropPath, optional SwiGLU FFN, LayerScale for residual scaling, and a Gram Loss for patch-level regularization.
